articles.title: Brain Secrets of SuperAgers: Why Some 80-Year-Olds Remember Like They're 50
articles.summary: Imagine being 80 years old with a memory as sharp as someone in their 50s. That's the reality for 'SuperAgers,' a group of remarkable seniors studied for over 25 years by researchers at Northwestern Medicine. These individuals, aged 80 and older, perform on memory tests as well as people 30 years younger, challenging the idea that mental decline is inevitable. Scientists discovered two key secrets in their brains: some SuperAgers are 'resistant,' lacking harmful proteins linked to Alzheimer's, while others are 'resilient,' having the proteins but still functioning normally. Their brains also have larger, healthier neurons in memory regions like the entorhinal cortex. Beyond biology, SuperAgers tend to be highly social and mentally active, building a 'cognitive reserve' that protects…

articles.title: Move Over, Chimps: This Cow Uses a Brush as a Tool — and Scientists Are Stunned
articles.summary: Meet Veronika, a Brown Swiss cow from Austria who is surprising scientists with a skill once thought unique to chimps: tool use. When she gets an itch, she picks up a long-handled brush with her tongue and scratches the exact spot. Even more amazing, she uses both ends — the hard bristles for her tough back and the soft handle for sensitive areas like her belly button. Animal cognition expert Antonio Osuna-Mascaró at the Messerli Research Institute in Vienna was stunned at first, but soon realized Veronika was deliberately choosing the right tool end. This makes her the first cow ever reported to use a tool, a finding published in Current Biology. Researchers think her life on a small farm…

articles.title: Dirty Air Crisis: Nearly Half of US Kids Breathing Dangerous Pollution, Report Warns
articles.summary: Imagine nearly half of all kids in the U.S. breathing air that could hurt their lungs. That's what a new report from the American Lung Association warns. The report, released on April 22, 2026, looked at pollution from smog and soot across the country. It found that 129.1 million people are breathing unhealthy air — the highest number in six years. Why is this happening? Climate change plays a big role, with extreme heat, drought, and wildfires making pollution worse. Even smoke from Canada's 2023 wildfires drifted into the U.S. There's also a surprising new source: datacenters, which use lots of electricity and often run on fossil fuels. The report says communities of color are hit hardest, and that the…
